title: Currency Pairs, In Plain English
body:
Currencies are always quoted in pairs, because money can only be valued in other money. GBP/USD at 1.2700 means one pound buys 1.27 dollars. That's it — everything else is vocabulary.

## Base and quote

The first currency in the pair is the base, the second is the quote. The price is always how much of the quote one unit of the base buys. When GBP/USD rises, the pound is strengthening against the dollar; when it falls, the dollar's winning the arm-wrestle.

## The families

The majors are the heavyweight pairs — the dollar against the pound, euro, yen, franc, and friends. Tightest costs, deepest markets, where beginners should live. Crosses skip the dollar entirely (EUR/GBP, GBP/JPY) — perfectly tradeable, slightly wilder. Exotics pair a major currency with a smaller economy's — wide costs, sudden lurches, genuinely not for your first year. Metals ride along too: gold quoted in dollars (XAU/USD) trades like a currency with a temper.

## Nicknames you'll hear

Cable for GBP/USD (after the Victorian telegraph cable that carried the rate across the Atlantic), the Fibre for EUR/USD, the Gopher for USD/JPY. Nobody needs these, but now the forums make sense.

## Why it matters

Every pair has a personality — how far it moves in a day, when it's busiest, what news yanks its chain. Our daily analysis covers 27 instruments precisely because a trader who knows their pair's habits has a head start on one who treats them all the same.

quiz.q: Which family should a beginner mostly trade?
'Cable' is trader slang for which pair?
